---
title: "Quarto 7 — Projects, Themes, Publishing"
subtitle: "From single files to deployed sites"
author: "R. Burke Squires"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Goals for this 20-minute block

Three short topics, each ~7 minutes:

- **Quarto projects** — what they are, the four types, when to use each.
- **Themes and CSS** — make your reports look like NIEHS reports.
- **Publishing** — get your work to a URL.

## 1. Quarto projects

A Quarto project is just a folder with a `_quarto.yml` file at its root. That file:

- Defines project-wide YAML defaults (apply to every document)
- Declares the project type (website, book, manuscript, default)
- Lets you render the whole project with one `quarto render`
- Centralizes resources (CSS, bibliography, images)

```
my-project/
├── _quarto.yml          ← Required. Defines the project.
├── index.qmd
├── about.qmd
├── chapters/
│   ├── ch01.qmd
│   └── ch02.qmd
├── references.bib
├── styles.css
└── _site/               ← Rendered output (auto-created)
```

This workshop is a Quarto **website** project. Look at the `_quarto.yml` at the root to see how it's wired together.

### The four project types

| Type | Use when |
|---|---|
| `default` | A folder of `.qmd` files that share resources but render independently |
| `website` | Multi-page site with navbar, sidebar, search (what this workshop uses) |
| `book` | Sequential pagination, downloadable PDF/EPUB, unified bibliography |
| `manuscript` | Scholarly article + supplementary analysis notebooks (Quarto 1.4+) |

### Quarto Manuscripts (worth knowing)

Added in Quarto 1.4 — designed for the scientific article use case where the manuscript **is** the analysis notebook. The output bundle includes the article PDF, an interactive HTML version, and the rendered analysis as supplementary material:

```yaml
project:
  type: manuscript

manuscript:
  article: index.qmd
  notebooks:
    - notebooks/figure-1.qmd
    - notebooks/supplementary-analysis.qmd
```

For NIEHS publishing workflows, this is the project type most worth investigating.

## 2. Themes and CSS

Quarto ships with all 25 Bootswatch themes — change the look of an entire site by changing one YAML value.

### Built-in themes

```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo     # or: flatly, journal, lumen, paper, simplex, sandstone,
                     # spacelab, united, yeti, cerulean, cyborg, darkly, ...
```

Try changing `theme: cosmo` to `theme: journal` in your project's `_quarto.yml` and re-rendering. The look changes everywhere.

::: {.callout-tip}
## R Markdown theme parity
The R Markdown `theme:` value (e.g., `flatly`) is the **same** name in Quarto. Themes you know from R Markdown work identically.
:::

### Dark mode

```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly
```

Quarto adds a sun/moon toggle to the navbar. Readers pick.

### Custom CSS

For specific tweaks beyond what themes offer:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, styles.css]
```

Then in `styles.css`:

```css
/* NIEHS forest green accent */
:root {
  --niehs-green: #205C40;
  --niehs-accent: #C77D34;
}

h1, h2, h3 {
  color: var(--niehs-green);
}

.callout-tip .callout-title {
  background-color: rgba(32, 92, 64, 0.1);
}

/* Custom block-quote style */
blockquote {
  border-left: 4px solid var(--niehs-green);
  padding-left: 1em;
  color: #475569;
}
```

This workshop's `styles.css` (in the project root) is exactly this pattern — Cosmo plus a minimal NIEHS overlay.

### SCSS for advanced theming

For deeper customization, create a `.scss` file with `defaults` and `rules` blocks:

```scss
/*-- scss:defaults --*/
$primary: #205C40;
$body-color: #2B2B2B;
$presentation-heading-color: $primary;

/*-- scss:rules --*/
.callout-note .callout-title {
  background-color: rgba(32, 92, 64, 0.1);
}
```

Reference it in YAML:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, niehs-theme.scss]
```

The afternoon slide decks use a `niehs-theme.scss` exactly this way — look at `afternoon_quarto/slides/niehs-theme.scss`.

### Conditional output across formats

Sometimes you want different content depending on the output format (an interactive table for HTML, a static one for Word). R Markdown uses `knitr::is_html_output()`. Quarto uses the same function:

```{r}
#| eval: false

if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
  DT::datatable(my_data)        # interactive HTML widget
} else {
  knitr::kable(my_data)         # static table for Word/PDF
}
```

`knitr::is_latex_output()`, `knitr::is_docx_output()`, and `knitr::pandoc_to()` cover other format checks.

## 3. Publishing

Quarto's `publish` subcommand handles deployment. Five reasonable targets.

### Target 0 — render and share the HTML

```bash
quarto render
```

Every `.qmd` becomes a standalone HTML file in `_site/`. These HTMLs are self-contained — you can:

- Email the file directly (single-doc reports work well).
- Drop the whole `_site/` folder on a shared drive (G:, OneDrive, SharePoint).
- Upload to an internal NIEHS web server.

For internal-only work, this is often the right answer. No accounts, no auth, no public URLs.

### Target 1 — Quarto Pub

Free, public hosting at `username.quarto.pub`:

```bash
quarto publish quarto-pub
```

First time: browser auth. Then one command forever.

### Target 2 — GitHub Pages

Push rendered site to a `gh-pages` branch:

```bash
quarto publish gh-pages
```

Site appears at `username.github.io/repo-name`. Requires the directory to be a Git repo with a GitHub remote.

### Target 3 — Posit Connect Cloud

Posit's hosted platform (free tier exists for static content; paid tier supports Shiny-Quarto interactivity):

```bash
quarto publish posit-connect-cloud
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Don't confuse with posit.cloud
**Publishing from posit.cloud was deprecated in Quarto 1.8.** Replacement is Posit Connect **Cloud** — a separate product despite the similar name. posit.cloud is still where you *develop*; only the publishing target changed.
:::

### Target 4 — static host (anywhere)

Render locally, copy `_site/` to Netlify, S3, Azure Static Web Apps, GitLab Pages — anywhere that serves HTML.

## Beyond today

Topics we touched but didn't cover deeply, with links for further reading:

::: {.incremental}
- **Quarto Dashboards** — fluid-layout interactive dashboards (Quarto 1.4+): <https://quarto.org/docs/dashboards/>
- **Quarto Extensions** — community shortcodes, filters, custom formats: <https://quarto.org/docs/extensions/>
- **Shiny + Quarto** — interactive documents backed by Shiny: <https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/shiny/>
- **Custom Pandoc filters** — when you need to bend the rendering pipeline: <https://quarto.org/docs/extensions/filters.html>
- **Quarto Manuscripts** — submitting to journals: <https://quarto.org/docs/manuscripts/>
:::

## Exercises

### Exercise 7.1 — Change the theme

1. Open `_quarto.yml` at the root of this workshop.
2. Find the `theme:` line.
3. Change `cosmo` to `journal`. Re-render. Compare.
4. Try `flatly`, `darkly`, `sandstone`. Pick a favorite.
5. Change back when done (or commit your preference).

### Exercise 7.2 — Render and share

In the Terminal:

```bash
quarto render
ls _site/
```

Confirm rendered HTML files exist in `_site/`. Open one in the browser. Imagine emailing it to a collaborator — that's "Target 0" publishing.

## What's next

The capstone. Build something of your own from scratch.

Open [`../capstone.qmd`](../capstone.qmd).
